beats.text: A time series is a sequence of observations indexed by time, where the order itself carries information.

visual.sub: Reading a series as structured parts plus leftover

beats.text: The classical additive decomposition splits the series into a trend, a seasonal pattern, and a remainder.
The trend is slow drift, the seasonal term repeats every m steps, and the remainder is what is left over.
When seasonal swings grow with the level of the series, a multiplicative form fits better, and logs make it additive again.

visual.note: Taking logs recovers the additive form

beats.text: Plug in a trend of 100, a seasonal term of 25, and a remainder of minus 5, and the observed value is 120.
The remainder is the part we want to be boring, because any leftover structure is what later chapters try to mop up.
A series is weakly stationary when its statistical character does not depend on when you look at it.

visual.sub: The assumption a clock loves to break

beats.text: Weak stationarity requires a constant mean, a constant variance, and a covariance that depends only on the gap.

visual.note: Mean and variance flat, covariance depends on lag k only

beats.text: A trend breaks the constant mean, widening swings break the constant variance, and a moving season breaks the covariance rule.
Ordinary data assumes rows are independent and identically distributed, but a time series is dependent by construction.
Stationarity is the weaker substitute: it does not require independence, only that the dependence structure stays stable.
A revenue series that climbs year after year has a rising mean, so it is not stationary in this sense.
Autocorrelation asks how dependent the points are and at what range by correlating the series with a delayed copy.
At lag k the autocorrelation is the covariance normalized by the variance, so it lives between minus one and plus one.
For the AR(1) process the autocorrelation decays geometrically as phi raised to the power k, a clean exponential fade.
The partial autocorrelation strips out the relayed paths and reports the direct dependence at range k.
An AR process shows a decaying ACF with a PACF that cuts off, while an MA process is the mirror image.

visual.tex: \text{AR}(p): \text{ACF decays},\ \text{PACF cuts at } p; \quad \text{MA}(q): \text{ACF cuts at } q,\ \text{PACF decays

beats.text: For an AR(1) with phi of 0.7, the theoretical autocorrelation at lag three is 0.7 cubed, which is 0.343.
Two reference processes anchor the subject: white noise as the picture of no structure, and the random walk.

visual.sub: The boring ideal and its cumulative cousin

beats.text: White noise is a sequence of uncorrelated, zero-mean, constant-variance shocks, stationary and unforecastable beyond its mean.

visual.note: Every autocorrelation past lag 0 is zero

beats.text: A random walk sets each value to the previous one plus a fresh shock, so it is the running sum of white noise.

visual.note: Variance grows as t times sigma squared

beats.text: It is the AR(1) pushed to its boundary at phi equals one, a unit root whose variance grows without bound.
Because variance climbs over time and there is no mean to revert to, a random walk is not stationary.
Distinguishing a true trend from a unit root is one of the field's defining problems, addressed by the Dickey-Fuller test.
Many real series are not stationary, so we apply cheap reversible transforms that attack the two ways stationarity fails.

visual.sub: Fixing a moving mean and a moving variance

beats.text: Differencing kills the trend by replacing the series with step-to-step changes, and one difference removes a linear trend.

visual.note: A second difference removes a quadratic trend

beats.text: Differencing a random walk returns pure white noise, annihilating the unit root in a single step.

visual.note: The non-stationary unit root is gone

beats.text: Differencing the series 2, 5, 9, 14 gives consecutive gaps of 3, 4, and 5, so the last value is five.
The Box-Cox family stabilizes variance with one tunable power, spanning no transform, square root, and log.

visual.note: lambda spans 1, 0.5, and the log limit

beats.text: The recipe is to stabilize variance first, then stabilize the mean with differencing, which feeds the I in ARIMA.
